topic: Introduction to Neurology / Investigations of the CNS

cards.q: State the two objectives of this lecture.
cards.a: To know the clinical approach to neurological cases via history taking and physical examination; and to know the relevant investigations.

cards.q: Define neurology.
cards.a: The branch of medicine concerned with the study and treatment of disorders of the nervous system.

cards.q: What does the nervous system do, and what does it comprise?
cards.a: It is a complex, sophisticated system that regulates and coordinates body activities; it comprises the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and the peripheral nerves.

cards.q: Name the four regions of the brain.
cards.a: The cerebrum; the diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus, subthalamus, epithalamus); the brainstem (midbrain, pons and medulla); and the cerebellum.

cards.q: Describe the cerebrum.
cards.a: The cerebral hemispheres constitute the greatest mass of brain tissue; the outer layers are formed by the cellular grey matter known as the cerebral cortex.

cards.q: What does consciousness depend on?
cards.a: Interaction between intact cerebral hemispheres and the activating (or arousal) system that lies in the diencephalon and upper brainstem.

cards.q: What two types of lesion may impair consciousness to the point of coma?
cards.a: Either extensive disease of the cerebral cortex, or damage to the activating system.

cards.q: What is the cerebellum primarily concerned with?
cards.a: Coordination and balance — it refines movements.

cards.q: What is the brainstem crucial for, and what are its three sections?
cards.a: Vital functions — breathing, heart rate, blood pressure and sleep-wake cycles; its three sections are the midbrain, pons and medulla.

cards.q: Describe the spinal cord and its two functions.
cards.a: A cylindrical mass of nervous tissue encased within the bony vertebral column; it contains long tracts connecting the brain with the peripheral nervous system, and it mediates reflex activity.

cards.q: What are the three basic components of a reflex?
cards.a: A receiving apparatus, a nerve centre, and a responding apparatus.

cards.q: List the conventional order of clerking in medicine.
cards.a: Presenting complaint; history of the presenting complaint; past medical history; past surgical history; pregnancy/perinatal/postnatal history; immunization; developmental milestones; family and social history; and drug history/allergy.

cards.q: What is described as the cornerstone of neurologic assessment, and who should the informants be?
cards.a: A detailed history; informants should be the parents, and older children (over 3–4 years) should be questioned.

cards.q: How should the history begin?
cards.a: With the chief complaint and its significance in the context of normal development.

cards.q: What six features should the history of the presenting complaint address?
cards.a: Location, quality, intensity, duration, associated features, and alleviating or exacerbating factors.

cards.q: Which pregnancy complications should be asked about in a neurological history?
cards.a: Pregnancy-induced hypertension, pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, vaginal bleeding, infections, and falls.

cards.q: What substances should be asked about in the pregnancy history?
cards.a: Drugs — prescription, herbal and illicit — and alcohol use.

cards.q: What does reduced or absent fetal movement suggest?
cards.a: Neuromuscular disorders.

cards.q: What delivery details should be recorded?
cards.a: Gestational age at birth and mode of delivery — SVD, vacuum- or forceps-assisted, or C/S (elective or emergency, and the indication); presence or absence of fetal distress; any resuscitation; birth weight; and any complicated hospital stay.

cards.q: If the infant had jaundice, what two things must be determined?
cards.a: Both the degree of jaundice and how it was managed.

cards.q: Name the four domains of development.
cards.a: Social, cognitive, language, and motor skills (fine and gross).

cards.q: Distinguish isolated from global developmental delay.
cards.a: Isolated delay affects one domain; global delay affects two or more domains.

cards.q: What does a static abnormality in development from birth suggest?
cards.a: A congenital, intrauterine, or perinatal cause.

cards.q: What does a loss of skills (regression) over time strongly suggest?
cards.a: An underlying degenerative disease of the CNS, such as an inborn error of metabolism.

cards.q: State the milestones at 3 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: supports weight on forearms. Fine motor: opens hands spontaneously. Social: smiles appropriately. Language: coos and laughs.

cards.q: State the milestones at 6 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: sits momentarily. Fine motor: transfers objects. Social: shows likes and dislikes. Language: babbles.

cards.q: State the milestones at 9 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: pulls to stand. Fine motor: pincer grasp. Social: plays pat-a-cake and peek-a-boo. Language: imitates sounds.

cards.q: State the milestones at 12 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: walks with 1 hand held. Fine motor: releases an object on command. Social: comes when called. Language: 1–2 meaningful words.

cards.q: State the milestones at 18 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: walks upstairs with assistance. Fine motor: feeds self from spoon. Social: mimics actions of others. Language: at least 6 words.

cards.q: State the milestones at 24 months.
cards.a: Gross motor: runs. Fine motor: builds a tower of 6 blocks. Social: plays with others. Language: 2- to 3-word sentences.

cards.q: What should the family history cover in neurology?
cards.a: History of neurologic disease including developmental delay and inherited disorders for all first- and second-degree relatives; miscarriages or fetal deaths (gender and gestational age at demise); and ethnic background, including whether the parents are related.

cards.q: Why is consanguinity important in a neurological history?
cards.a: Because the incidence of metabolic and degenerative disorders of the CNS is increased significantly in children of consanguineous marriages.

cards.q: What should the social history detail?
cards.a: The child's current living environment and relationship with family members; recent stressors such as divorce, remarriage, birth of a sibling or death of a loved one; academic and social performance with attention to abrupt changes; and peer relationships.

cards.q: What may it indicate if a child cannot name at least two or three playmates?
cards.a: Abnormal social development.

cards.q: Why is a review of systems essential in neurology?
cards.a: Because abnormalities of the CNS often manifest with vague, non-focal symptoms that may be misattributed to other organ systems — e.g. vomiting, constipation and urinary incontinence.

cards.q: How should the physical examination of a child be conducted?
cards.a: In a non-threatening, child-friendly setting, allowing the child to sit where most comfortable — on a parent's lap or on the floor.

cards.q: Which tests should be reserved for the end of the examination, and why?
cards.a: Invasive, painful or discomforting tests such as measurement of head circumference and the gag reflex — because the more the examination seems like a game, the more the child will cooperate.

cards.q: What does general observation include?
cards.a: The child's appearance, behaviour, mental state, abnormal posture or motor function, and the head — size, shape, fontanels and OFC.

cards.q: When does the examination begin?
cards.a: During the interview — observing the child's appearance and movements, dysmorphic facies, abnormality of motor function such as hemiparesis or gait disturbance, and behaviour while playing.

cards.q: What is the normal pattern of a child's behaviour during a consultation?
cards.a: A normal child usually plays independently early in the visit but then engages in the interview process.

cards.q: What does microcephaly reflect, and what causes it?
cards.a: A small head frequently reflects a small brain; the cause is a perinatal or postnatal insult to the brain.

cards.q: What are the causes of macrocephaly?
cards.a: Most commonly familial; but it may be from a disturbance of growth, chromosomal defect, storage disorder, or hydrocephalus.

cards.q: What is craniosynostosis?
cards.a: Premature closure of cranial sutures, causing a variety of abnormal head shapes.

cards.q: What head shape suggests chronic subdural haemorrhage, and why?
cards.a: A square or box-like shape, due to the long-standing presence of fluid in the subdural space.

cards.q: What is plagiocephaly, and in which infants is it particularly prominent?
cards.a: Flattening of the skull; it can be seen in normal infants but may be particularly prominent in hypotonic or weak infants, who are less mobile.

cards.q: Describe the anterior fontanel.
cards.a: Diamond-shaped, at the junction of the frontal and parietal bones, measuring 2 × 2 cm, and closing at 9–18 months.

cards.q: Describe the posterior fontanel.
cards.a: Triangular in shape, at the junction of the parietal and occipital bones, closing at 6–8 weeks.

cards.q: What does a bulging fontanel indicate, and what is the important caveat?
cards.a: Increased intracranial pressure — but it also occurs with vigorous crying in a normal infant.

cards.q: What might a very small or absent anterior fontanel at birth indicate?
cards.a: Craniosynostosis or microcephaly.

cards.q: What does the occipitofrontal circumference reflect?
cards.a: Brain growth — it should be measured and documented.

cards.q: State the average head circumference of a term infant at birth and its rate of growth in the first year.
cards.a: 34–35 cm at birth; increasing by 2 cm/month for the 1st 3 months, 1 cm/month for the 2nd 3 months, and 0.5 cm/month for the last 6 months.

cards.q: State the rate of head growth after the first year.
cards.a: 0.25 cm/month from 1–3 years, then 0.5 cm/year up to 6 years; after 6 years it ceases to be relevant.

cards.q: State the average rate of head growth in a healthy premature infant.
cards.a: 0.5 cm in the first 2 weeks, 0.75 cm in the 3rd week, and 1.0 cm in the 4th week and every week thereafter until the 40th week of development.

cards.q: List the eight steps in the sequence of central nervous system examination.
cards.a: Higher function; cranial nerves; motor functions (posture, nutrition of muscle, DTR, power, superficial reflexes); sensory; autonomic function; soft neurologic signs; cerebellar function; and gait.

cards.q: What is assessed under sensory examination?
cards.a: Light touch, pain, temperature, joint position, vibration, stereognosis, and Romberg's sign.

cards.q: What is assessed under autonomic function and soft neurologic signs?
cards.a: Autonomic: rest and exercise pulse and blood pressure. Soft neurologic signs: neck stiffness, Kernig's sign and Brudzinski's sign.

cards.q: What is assessed under cerebellar function?
cards.a: Intention tremor, nystagmus and dysdiadochokinesia.

cards.q: Name the six gait patterns listed.
cards.a: Hemiparetic; ataxic (cerebellar and sensory); spastic; steppage; myopathic; and waddling.

cards.q: On what will the examiner's assessment of candidates be based?
cards.a: Overall attention to sequence, composure, and speed of performance.

cards.q: List the five instruments for neurologic examination.
cards.a: Inelastic measuring tape, pen torch, tuning fork (256 Hz), tendon hammer, and cotton wool.

cards.q: State the levels of consciousness.
cards.a: Conscious, lethargic, obtundation, stupor, and coma.

cards.q: Distinguish aphasia from dysarthria.
cards.a: Aphasia is inability to speak, due to damage to Broca's area; dysarthria is inability to speak properly, due to damage to the articulation system.

cards.q: What else is assessed under higher function?
cards.a: Intelligence and memory (alertness in a younger child, simple arithmetic in an older child); orientation of time, place and person; and cerebral dominance — most people are left.

cards.q: Name the twelve cranial nerves in order.
cards.a: Olfactory, optic, oculomotor, trochlear, trigeminal, abducent, facial, auditory, glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory, and hypoglossal.

cards.q: Which cranial nerves are tested by visual acuity, fields, colour vision and fundi, and by pupillary reactions?
cards.a: Visual acuity, field of vision, colour vision and ocular fundi test CN 2; pupillary reactions test CN 2 and 3.

cards.q: Which cranial nerves are tested by extraocular movements, and by corneal reflexes and jaw movements?
cards.a: Extraocular movements test CN 3, 4 and 6; corneal reflexes and jaw movements test CN 5.

cards.q: Which cranial nerves are tested by facial movements, hearing, swallowing and palate, voice, speech and tongue movement?
cards.a: Facial movements CN 7; hearing CN 8; swallowing and rise of palate CN 9 and 10; voice CN 10; speech CN 5, 7, 10 and 12; tongue movement CN 12.

cards.q: State the four tests used to assess muscle tone and the findings in hypotonia.
cards.a: Palpation of muscles — flabby; posture of limb — limp; resistance to passive movement — decreased; range of passive movement — increased.

cards.q: State the findings of the four tone tests in hypertonia.
cards.a: Palpation — rigid; posture of limb — stiff; resistance to passive movement — increased; range of passive movement — decreased.

cards.q: Define opisthotonos and state when it occurs.
cards.a: Severe hyperextension of the spine caused by hypertonia of the paraspinal muscles; it may be exhibited by patients with either spasticity or rigidity.

cards.q: State the muscle power grading scale from 0 to 5.
cards.a: 0 none; 1 flickering movement; 2 movement possible after elimination of gravity; 3 movement possible against gravity but not against resistance; 4 movement possible against gravity and against some resistance; 5 normal power.

cards.q: State the reflex grading scale.
cards.a: 0 absent; 1+ sluggish (seen with reinforcement); 2+ normal; 3+ brisk; 4+ brisk with clonus.

cards.q: Define lumbar puncture and give its other name.
cards.a: A clinical procedure in which a needle is inserted into the spinal canal, most commonly to collect CSF for diagnostic testing; also known as a spinal tap.

cards.q: State three procedural principles of lumbar puncture.
cards.a: It is invasive and hence requires consent; strict asepsis is required; and it can also be performed for therapeutic purposes.

cards.q: List the nine requirements for the LP procedure.
cards.a: Indications; rule out contraindications; vital signs; pre-procedure concomitant RBS; position; needle insertion point and needle type; asepsis; pain control; and specimen collection.

cards.q: List the indications for lumbar puncture.
cards.a: Meningitis; encephalitis (autoimmune, infectious); idiopathic intracranial hypertension; and it is often helpful in assessing subarachnoid haemorrhage, demyelinating, degenerative and collagen vascular diseases, and intracranial neoplasms.

cards.q: List the contraindications to lumbar puncture.
cards.a: Suspected mass lesion of the brain, especially posterior fossa or above the tentorium causing midline shift; suspected mass lesion of the spinal cord; symptoms and signs of impending cerebral herniation in a child with probable meningitis; critical illness; skin infection at the site; and thrombocytopenia with a platelet count below 20 × 10⁹/L.

cards.q: Describe the position for lumbar puncture and the reason for it.
cards.a: Lateral decubitus or seated position, with neck and legs flexed to enlarge the intervertebral spaces, while shoulders and hips are kept straight to prevent rotating the spine.

cards.q: Where is the needle inserted for a lumbar puncture, and how is the site identified?
cards.a: At the L3–L4 or L4–L5 interspace, identified by drawing an imaginary line from the iliac crest downward, perpendicular to the vertebral column.

cards.q: Describe the asepsis required for lumbar puncture.
cards.a: The physician dons a mask, gown and sterile gloves; the skin is thoroughly prepared with a cleansing agent, and sterile drapes are applied.

cards.q: Describe pain control for lumbar puncture.
cards.a: Injecting a local anaesthetic such as 1% lidocaine at the time of the procedure, or applying a eutectic mixture of lidocaine and prilocaine (EMLA) to the skin 30 minutes before the procedure.

cards.q: Describe the needle used for lumbar puncture and how it is introduced.
cards.a: A 22-gauge, 1.5- to 3.0-inch, sharp, bevelled spinal needle with a properly fitting stylet, introduced in the midsagittal plane and directed slightly cephalad.

cards.q: What is the diagnostic utility of skull X-rays?
cards.a: Limited — they can demonstrate fractures, bony defects, intracranial calcifications, or indirect evidence of increased intracranial pressure.

cards.q: What is cranial ultrasonography the imaging method of choice for, and in whom?
cards.a: For detecting intracranial haemorrhage, periventricular leukomalacia and hydrocephalus — in infants with patent anterior fontanels.

cards.q: What is cranial CT valuable for?
cards.a: The evaluation of many neurologic emergencies, as well as some non-emergent conditions.

cards.q: What is brain MRI well suited for?
cards.a: It is a non-invasive procedure well suited for detecting a variety of abnormalities, including those of the posterior fossa and spinal cord.

cards.q: Name the other neuroradiologic procedures.
cards.a: Cranial CT angiography, MR angiography and MR venography; proton MR spectroscopy; catheter angiography; and positron emission tomography.

cards.q: What unique information does PET provide?
cards.a: Information on brain metabolism and perfusion, by measuring blood flow, oxygen uptake, and/or glucose consumption.

cards.q: What is EEG used for?
cards.a: The diagnosis of epilepsy and brain dysfunction.

cards.q: Define an evoked potential.
cards.a: An electrical signal recorded from the CNS following the presentation of a specific visual, auditory, or sensory stimulus.

cards.q: How are children with intellectual disability or developmental delay often evaluated?
cards.a: With metabolic and/or genetic testing.

cards.q: OSPE — A child has always been behind but has never lost a skill. What does this pattern indicate?
cards.a: A static abnormality in development from birth — suggesting a congenital, intrauterine or perinatal cause.

cards.q: OSPE — A child developed normally then began losing skills across several domains. What does this indicate and what investigation is needed?
cards.a: Global developmental regression, suggesting a degenerative CNS disease such as an inborn error of metabolism — requiring metabolic and/or genetic testing.

cards.q: OSPE — What single question best detects developmental regression?
cards.a: 'Is there anything he used to be able to do that he cannot do now?'

cards.q: OSPE — An infant's head circumference is crossing centile lines upward. Why is this different from a single large measurement?
cards.a: Because macrocephaly is most commonly familial, but a head crossing centiles indicates something actively expanding inside the skull — it cannot be familial.

cards.q: OSPE — How should a fontanel be assessed to avoid a false positive?
cards.a: With the infant calm and sitting up — because vigorous crying and lying flat both cause the fontanel to bulge in a normal infant.

cards.q: OSPE — A drowsy child with meningitis has a unilaterally dilated pupil, hypertension and bradycardia. What is happening and what must NOT be done?
cards.a: Impending cerebral herniation with raised intracranial pressure — lumbar puncture is contraindicated, as it may precipitate coning and brainstem compression.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does a unilaterally dilated pupil occur in raised intracranial pressure?
cards.a: Because the temporal lobe is pushed down over the tentorium and compresses the third cranial nerve, whose parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the combination of hypertension with bradycardia in raised ICP called, and why does it occur?
cards.a: Cushing's response — blood pressure rises to maintain cerebral perfusion against the raised intracranial pressure, and baroreceptors reflexly slow the heart.

cards.q: OSPE — In a child with meningitis and signs of raised ICP, what is the correct order of management?
cards.a: Give empirical intravenous antibiotics immediately; stabilise airway, breathing and circulation; obtain urgent cranial CT; and defer the lumbar puncture. Never delay antibiotics for an LP.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must a lumbar puncture never be performed when there is a mass lesion or raised ICP?
cards.a: Because removing CSF from below creates a pressure gradient across the foramen magnum, forcing the brainstem downwards — coning — which compresses the centres for breathing, heart rate and blood pressure.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is a random blood sugar taken before a lumbar puncture?
cards.a: Because CSF glucose is interpreted as a ratio to the blood glucose taken at the same time; without it, the CSF glucose result cannot be interpreted.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is the LP performed at L3–L4 or L4–L5 and not higher?
cards.a: Because the spinal cord ends at about L1–L2; below that level there are only the loose nerve roots of the cauda equina, which move out of the needle's way.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must the stylet be kept in the needle during skin puncture?
cards.a: To prevent coring a plug of skin into the spinal canal, which can later grow into an epidermoid tumour.

cards.q: OSPE — An infant with an open fontanel needs brain imaging for suspected hydrocephalus. Which investigation and why?
cards.a: Cranial ultrasonography — the fontanel provides an acoustic window; it uses no radiation, is portable, needs no sedation and can be repeated for serial monitoring.

cards.q: OSPE — Why can cranial ultrasound not be used once the fontanel has closed?
cards.a: Because ultrasound cannot pass through bone; the open fontanel is the only acoustic window into the infant brain.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is CT preferred to MRI in a neurological emergency?
cards.a: Because CT is fast, widely available, usually needs no sedation, and shows acute blood and bone well — whereas MRI takes far longer and often requires sedation in a child.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is MRI superior to CT for the posterior fossa?
cards.a: Because CT is degraded by artefact from the dense surrounding bone, whereas MRI uses a magnetic field and is unaffected by bone — important since most childhood brain tumours are in the posterior fossa.

cards.q: OSPE — Why can imaging not diagnose epilepsy?
cards.a: Because epilepsy is a disorder of electrical activity while imaging shows structure; the brain may look entirely normal, so an EEG is required.

cards.q: OSPE — Which combination of tone and reflexes indicates an upper motor neurone lesion?
cards.a: Increased tone with brisk reflexes (3+ or 4+ with clonus) — indicating a lesion in the brain or spinal cord.

cards.q: OSPE — Which combination of tone and reflexes indicates a lower motor neurone lesion?
cards.a: Decreased tone with absent or reduced reflexes — indicating a lesion in the anterior horn, nerve or muscle.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you distinguish power grade 2 from grade 3?
cards.a: Grade 2 can move the limb only with gravity eliminated (horizontally); grade 3 can lift the limb against gravity but not against resistance.

cards.q: OSPE — What is reinforcement, and why does it matter in grading reflexes?
cards.a: Asking the patient to clench the teeth or pull interlocked fingers apart while the reflex is tested; a reflex present only with reinforcement is graded 1+, not 0 — and a truly absent reflex means the reflex arc is broken.

cards.q: OSPE — What is Romberg's sign, and what does a positive sign indicate?
cards.a: The patient stands steady with eyes open but becomes unsteady with eyes closed — indicating sensory ataxia rather than cerebellar ataxia, in which the patient is unsteady with eyes open as well.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A mother reports reduced fetal movements during pregnancy. What does this suggest and why?
cards.a: A neuromuscular disorder — because fetal movement requires functioning nerves and muscles, so the mother was in effect observing the neurological examination in utero.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must the indication for an emergency caesarean section be asked, not just the mode of delivery?
cards.a: Because the indication is often the neurological event itself — fetal distress, cord prolapse or abruption.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child with a movement disorder and deafness was 'very yellow' as a newborn and untreated. What is the likely diagnosis?
cards.a: Kernicterus — which is why the birth history must establish both the degree of jaundice and how it was managed.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child presents with recurrent early morning vomiting and has been treated repeatedly for gastritis. What neurological cause must be excluded?
cards.a: Raised intracranial pressure — CNS disease often manifests with vague, non-focal symptoms misattributed to other systems.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A previously dry child becomes incontinent of urine. What neurological cause should be considered?
cards.a: A spinal cord lesion — since urinary incontinence is listed among the non-focal symptoms of CNS disease.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does headache worse on lying down, coughing or straining matter?
cards.a: Because these manoeuvres raise intracranial pressure, so the pattern suggests raised ICP — it is elicited by asking about alleviating and exacerbating factors.
